In 2020, White, non-Hispanic individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, individuals aged 25-34 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for new court commitments, while individuals aged 45-54 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for parole returns and revocations.

## Prison Admissions
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.



Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In West Virginia in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 100% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 0%.


``` ::: :::



::: citation-section **Citations** Alper, Mariel E., et al. ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Incarcerated felons of a state correctional institution generally become eligible for parole when they have served the minimum term of an indeterminate sentence or one-quarter of the term of a definite sentence. Some inmates may qualify for an accelerated parole program if they meet certain criteria, apply, and are accepted into the program. The accelerated parole program allows for release eligibility up to three months prior to the original parole eligibility date, contingent on completion of a case management plan tailored to criminogenic needs. In addition to meeting the time requirements for parole, inmates are not eligible for release if they are currently in restrictive housing for a disciplinary violation.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population Currently Eligible for Parole

Show breakdown of types:
1) New court commitments/1-24.99 years
2) New court commitments/<1 & >= 25 years
3) Parole returns/1-24.99 years
4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Focusing on New Court Commitments with
Sentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 1,322 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 33% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Demographics

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset .tab2} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, White, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 88 percent.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 88 percent.


``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 43 percent.


``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with property offenses constituted the majority, representing 37 percent.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 10 to 24.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 60 percent.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Missing Data

This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state once new NCRP data is released.

``` ::: :::

## Prison Releases
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Release Trends

Sentence about change in releases from 2010 to 2020.



This chart shows an annual breakdown from 2010 to 2020 on the proportion of the parole-eligible population that was released each year compared to those who remained incarcerated despite their parole eligibility.



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Release Types

Sentence about conditional and unconditional releases.




``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Demographics

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset .tab3} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence about race and ethnicity.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence about gender.


``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence about age at release.



``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Timing of Release

This chart illustrates the timing of releases in relation to parole eligibility year by release type in 2020, comparing conditional and unconditional releases. It shows the proportion of individuals released before their parole eligibility year, during the year they were eligible, within one to five years after eligibility, more than five years after, and those with missing parole eligibility data.



This graphic depicts the distribution of release timings for different types of offenses in 2020. It shows the percentages of individuals released at various intervals relative to their parole eligibility year, ranging from those released before their parole eligibility year to those who remained incarcerated more than five years after becoming eligible for parole.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence about the longest time served by offense type.


``` ::: :::
